name: Mirandese

ancestors.notes: Old Portuguese (Aviz-period) adstrate: as Portuguese consolidated as the state language, the Leonese-speaking communities of Miranda, Rionor, and Guadramil faced intensifying pressure. Portuguese orthographic conventions (lh, nh, ã) penetrated the writing tradition.

notes: Mirandese (mirandés), spoken in the Miranda do Douro municipality, northeastern Portugal. Classified as Asturleonese. Recognised as a regional/minority language in Portugal since 1999. Retains archaic Asturleonese features: diphthongisation of Latin short /e/ and /o/ (written ⟨iê/ie⟩, ⟨uô/uo⟩), palatal lateral /ʎ/ preserved (written ⟨lh⟩ in all positions; word-initial ⟨l⟩ before vowel = /ʎ/ in traditional lexicon per Convenção § L). Heavy Portuguese adstrate via official education and administration. Mirandese preserves the medieval four-way+ sibilant contrast that Portuguese lost, the hallmark of the language: (1) voiceless apico-alveolar /s̺/ ⟨s, ss⟩ and its voiced intervocalic counterpart /z̺/ ⟨-s-⟩; (2) voiceless (pre)dorso-dental /s/ ⟨c (before e/i), ç⟩ and voiced /z/ ⟨z⟩; (3) postalveolar /ʃ/ ⟨x⟩ and /ʒ/ ⟨j, g (before e/i)⟩; (4) the affricate /tʃ/ ⟨ch⟩ (still an affricate, unlike modern European Portuguese). NOTATION CONVENTION (matches the TigreGotico/mirandese_g2p expert gold): the apical/dorsal CONTRAST is written by marking only the apical series with the ̺ diacritic (⟨s⟩ = /s̺/, /z̺/) and leaving the dorso-dental series unmarked (⟨c, ç, z⟩ = plain /s/, /z/); this is IPA-standard, keeps the phonemic contrast explicit, and avoids the over-narrow laminal ̻ diacritic the gold does not use. ⟨ç⟩ is a fricative, not an affricate, and occurs word-finally (rapaç, lhuç) where Portuguese writes -z. ⟨j⟩ is /ʒ/ (western Iberian, never the Asturian palatal ʝ) — ⟨y⟩ is the glide /j/. INITIAL-L PALATALISATION (Leonese): word-initial ⟨l⟩ before a vowel = /ʎ/ (luç → [ˈʎus], Convenção § L; bare ⟨l⟩ /l/ survives only in loanwords, proper nouns and the article — a documented residual limit); ⟨lh⟩ = /ʎ/ and ⟨nh⟩ = /ɲ/. The trigraphs ⟨anh, enh, inh, onh, unh⟩ are given explicitly (oral vowel + /ɲ/, e.g. danho → [ˈdaɲu]) so the greedy nasal-vowel digraph does not swallow the /ɲ/ of a following ⟨nh⟩. Nasal digraphs ⟨an, en, in, on, un⟩ realise Portuguese-adstrate nasal vowels /ɐ̃ ẽ ĩ õ ũ/ before a consonant or at word boundary (brando → [ˈbɾɐ̃du]); before a vowel they are oral vowel + /n/. ALLOPHONY (post-lexical, allophone_rules): coda /n/ assimilates in place to a following labial ([m]) or velar ([ŋ]). Ibero-Romance intervocalic voiced-stop spirantisation (⟨b d g⟩ → [β ð ɣ]) is a REAL native process (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000) but is LEXICALLY VARIABLE in the gold, which is stop-dominant (b:54/β:13, d:41/ð:12) because the attested wordlist is loan-heavy and conservatively transcribed; a blanket spirantisation rule regresses this expert gold, so the spirants are declared in the `allophones` inventory (available to the lattice / downstream engines) but NOT applied as a default rewrite — realising them is left to the downstream mwl_phonemizer where lexical conditioning is available.

sources.author: Vasconcelos, J. Leite de
sources.title: Estudos de Philologia Mirandesa
sources.notes: Foundational description of Mirandese phonology, including the six-sibilant system.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: Official orthography. § L: word-initial l before vowel = /ʎ/ in traditional lexicon (bare l occurs only in loanwords/proper nouns/article). § Nasalidade: an/en/in/on/un are nasal-vowel digraphs at word boundaries; they do NOT nasalise sequences before onset consonants. § Sibilantes: six-way contrast s̺/z̺/s̻/z̻/ʃ/ʒ. § Ditongos: iê/ie = /je/ (stressed /jɛ/); uô/uo = /wo/ (stressed /wɔ/). § Acento: paroxytone default; oxytone for -r/-l/-z/-ç/-in/-un/-on/-is/-us/-ns/-ão endings.

sources.author: Ferreira, M.B. & Raposo, D. (coords.)
sources.title: Primeiro Aditamento à Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa
sources.publisher: Câmara Municipal de Miranda do Douro / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa
sources.notes: February 2000 addendum. Key provision: Sendinese speakers may optionally write word-initial l- (not lh-) to represent /l/ rather than /ʎ/ (e.g., luna/lhuna). Sendinese monophthongisation of iê→i and uô→u also codified. Source: secondary (studosmirandeses.blogs.sapo.pt, cursodemirandes.wordpress.com); standalone PDF not located.

stress.notes: Convenção Ortográfica da Língua Mirandesa (1999) accentuation, Portuguese-based: paroxytone default; written accent (acute or tilde) overrides. Oxytone-attracting endings reflect Mirandese orthography: consonant-final r/l/z/ç (rapaç, Pertual, falar) and the final nasals written with -n in the Asturleonese manner — -in (camin, Sendin), -un, and the systematically oxytone -on class from Latin -ōnem (naçon, coraçon, Associaçon). Unmarked -an/-en verb endings stay paroxytone as in Portuguese -am/-em.

allophone_rules.notes: Nasal place assimilation (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000 §3; general Ibero-Romance): a coda /n/ agrees in place with a following labial stop/nasal, surfacing as [m].
Nasal place assimilation (Mateus & d'Andrade 2000 §3): a coda /n/ agrees in place with a following velar stop, surfacing as [ŋ].
